<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Deformation theory</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},

      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  /* sticky sidebar TOC (wide screens only; narrow screens fall back to top-nav) */
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>

rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 First-order deformations 2 Tangent space of moduli is $H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$ 3 Obstructions live in $H^2(X_0, T_{X_0})$ 4 Deformation functors and Schlessinger's criteria 5 The cotangent complex $L_{X/Y}$ 6 Worked example: deformations of a smooth curve

Deformation theory

First-order deformations live in $H^1(T)$, obstructions in $H^2(T)$ — the cotangent complex unifies them.

1. First-order deformations

Let $k$ be a field and $X_0$ a scheme over $k$ — for now think of an affine variety, but the definition is local and globalises with no friction. Write $D = k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ for the ring of dual numbers; geometrically $\Spec\,D$ is a "fattened point", a single closed point with a one-dimensional tangent direction. A first-order deformation of $X_0$ is a flat $D$-scheme $X$ together with a Cartesian square

$$\begin{array}{ccc} X_0 & \hookrightarrow & X \\ \downarrow & & \downarrow \\ \Spec\,k & \hookrightarrow & \Spec\,D \end{array}$$

identifying the special fibre $X \otimes_D k$ with $X_0$. Flatness is the key word: it is what stops $X$ from being just a disjoint pair of fibres or an arbitrary thickening, and it is the algebraic incarnation of "the family deforms continuously". Two first-order deformations $X, X'$ are equivalent if there is a $D$-isomorphism $X \to X'$ restricting to the identity on $X_0$. The set of equivalence classes is denoted $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}(D)$, or simply $T^1_{X_0}$.

Why is the dual-number axis the right "tangent direction"? Functorially, a $D$-point of any moduli space $\mathcal{M}$ is a pair (a $k$-point $[X_0]$, plus a tangent vector at $[X_0]$). So $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}(D)$ literally is the tangent space to the moduli problem at $X_0$ — provided we have a moduli space at all. (Hartshorne, Sernesi, and Illusie all open with this observation; it is the gateway to every later structure theorem.)

The simplest non-example shows what flatness does. Take $X_0 = \Spec\,k[x,y]/(xy)$, the node. The trivial deformation is $X_0 \times_k \Spec\,D = \Spec\,k[x,y,\epsilon]/(xy, \epsilon^2)$ — a node with a fattened point glued to it but no actual smoothing. Replacing $xy$ by $xy - \epsilon$ gives a different deformation $\Spec\,k[x,y,\epsilon]/(xy - \epsilon, \epsilon^2)$, which is again flat over $D$ but now not equivalent to the trivial one — it is the first-order trace of smoothing the node to a hyperbola $xy = t$. Cohomology will see this as a non-zero class in $H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$; the proof-scrubber below is the bridge.

Worked example: for $X_0 = \Spec\,A_0$ affine smooth over $k$, every first-order deformation is trivial — equivalently, $T^1_{X_0} = H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$ vanishes for $X_0$ affine. The Čech computation behind this is exactly the obstruction-free patching the scrubber walks through. The interesting examples are global (projective) or singular.

See also
  • Flat modules
  • Prime spectrum and Zariski topology

2. Tangent space of moduli is $H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$

Section 1 ended on the punchline that $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}(D) \cong H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$ for $X_0$ smooth (or, more generally, locally complete intersection with controlled singularities). This section unpacks why this is the tangent space to moduli: the construction is the standard "patching" pattern, and exhibiting the bijection on a small example is the right way to internalise it.

The setup. Choose an affine open cover $\{U_i\}$ of $X_0$ with $X_0$ smooth. Flatness of any $D$-deformation $X$ is a local condition; on each $U_i$, smoothness gives a trivialisation $\varphi_i\colon X|_{U_i} \xrightarrow{\sim} U_i \times_k \Spec\,D$ (Hartshorne III.9.9). On $U_i \cap U_j$ we have two such trivialisations, and their difference $\varphi_j \circ \varphi_i^{-1}$ is an automorphism of $(U_i \cap U_j) \times \Spec\,D$ that restricts to the identity modulo $\epsilon$. Such an automorphism is precisely $\mathrm{id} + \epsilon \cdot \theta_{ij}$ for a unique derivation $\theta_{ij} \in T_{X_0}(U_i \cap U_j)$. The cocycle condition $\theta_{ij} + \theta_{jk} = \theta_{ik}$ on triples is forced; coboundaries arise from changes of trivialisation. Hence equivalence classes of first-order deformations $\leftrightarrow \check H^1(\{U_i\}, T_{X_0}) = H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$.

The geometric content: a class in $H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$ encodes the obstruction to gluing the locally trivial pieces back into a globally trivial deformation. For $X_0 = \mathbb{P}^1$, $T_{\mathbb{P}^1} = \mathcal{O}(2)$ and $H^1(\mathbb{P}^1, \mathcal{O}(2)) = 0$ — every first-order deformation of $\mathbb{P}^1$ is trivial, i.e. $\mathbb{P}^1$ is infinitesimally rigid. The same vanishing $H^1(\mathbb{P}^n, T_{\mathbb{P}^n}) = 0$ holds for all $n \ge 1$ via the Euler sequence; $\mathbb{P}^n$ has no moduli. By contrast for a smooth curve $C$ of genus $g \ge 2$, Riemann–Roch and Serre duality give $\dim H^1(C, T_C) = 3g - 3$, the classical dimension of $\mathcal{M}_g$.

Worked example. For an elliptic curve $E$ over $k = \bar k$, $T_E \cong \mathcal{O}_E$ (the tangent bundle of an abelian variety is trivial), so $H^1(E, T_E) \cong H^1(E, \mathcal{O}_E)$ has dimension $1$ — recovering the familiar fact that $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is one-dimensional, parametrised by the $j$-invariant.

3. Obstructions live in $H^2(X_0, T_{X_0})$

First-order deformations are the linear shadow of moduli; to "integrate" them — to extend a first-order lift over $D = k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ to a second-order lift over $k[t]/(t^3)$, then $k[t]/(t^4)$, and onwards — generally requires solving a non-linear cocycle equation, and the obstruction to doing so lives one degree higher: in $H^2(X_0, T_{X_0})$.

The mechanism. Start with a flat lift $X_n$ over $A_n = k[t]/(t^{n+1})$. Try to extend it to a lift $X_{n+1}$ over $A_{n+1} = k[t]/(t^{n+2})$. Locally — on each affine $U_i$ in your cover — extensions exist freely; the local automorphism groups are torsors over $T_{X_0}(U_i)$, exactly as before. Compatible local extensions glue iff a 2-cocycle $\mathrm{ob}(X_n) \in C^2(\{U_i\}, T_{X_0})$ vanishes; its cohomology class $[\mathrm{ob}(X_n)] \in H^2(X_0, T_{X_0})$ is the obstruction. If $[\mathrm{ob}(X_n)] = 0$ the obstruction can be killed by re-choosing the local extensions, and a global lift $X_{n+1}$ exists. If not, no $X_{n+1}$ exists; the deformation is obstructed at order $n$.

Two consequences worth memorising. (a) Unobstructed implies smooth moduli. If $H^2(X_0, T_{X_0}) = 0$ then every obstruction class is forced to vanish and every first-order deformation extends to all orders, giving a formal smoothness statement: $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ is pro-represented by a power-series ring $k[\![t_1, \ldots, t_d]\!]$ with $d = \dim H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$. (b) Curves are special. For a smooth projective curve $C$ the dimension constraint $\dim C = 1$ forces $H^2(C, T_C) = 0$ (any sheaf on a 1-dimensional space has no $H^{\ge 2}$); this is why $\mathcal{M}_g$ is smooth and why curves "have no obstructions" — the deeper reason behind the dimension count $3g-3$.

Counter-classical examples. Calabi–Yau threefolds typically have non-zero $H^2(X, T_X)$ but a miraculous cancellation (Bogomolov–Tian–Todorov, Ran's $T^1$-lifting): the obstruction map vanishes nonetheless, and the deformation theory is unobstructed despite having non-trivial $H^2$. By contrast, some threefolds have genuinely obstructed deformations — Mukai-style classifying spaces with non-reduced moduli.

Worked example. Consider $X_0 = \mathbb{P}^n$. The Euler sequence $0 \to \mathcal{O} \to \mathcal{O}(1)^{n+1} \to T_{\mathbb{P}^n} \to 0$ shows $H^i(\mathbb{P}^n, T_{\mathbb{P}^n}) = 0$ for all $i \ge 1$. Hence $\mathbb{P}^n$ is rigid (no first-order deformations) and unobstructed (no obstructions to chase). The "moduli space" of $\mathbb{P}^n$'s is a single point.

4. Deformation functors and Schlessinger's criteria

The two preceding sections gave a hands-on Čech construction. To talk about moduli abstractly — even when no actual moduli scheme exists — Grothendieck and Schlessinger formalised the data into a functor.

Let $\mathrm{Art}_k$ denote the category of Artin local $k$-algebras with residue field $k$ (so $A$ is finite-dimensional over $k$ and has a unique maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m}_A$ with $A/\mathfrak{m}_A = k$). The deformation functor of $X_0$ is

$\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}\colon \mathrm{Art}_k \to \mathrm{Set}, \quad A \;\longmapsto\; \{\text{flat lifts $X/\Spec\,A$ of $X_0$}\}/\!\sim$

where $\sim$ is $A$-isomorphism fixing the special fibre $X_0$. The dual numbers $D = k[\epsilon]/(\epsilon^2)$ are an Artin local ring, so $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}(D) \cong H^1(X_0, T_{X_0})$ from §1–2 fits cleanly into this language.

The functor $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ is rarely representable in the strict sense (it would require a fine moduli scheme; deformations have automorphisms which obstruct this). Schlessinger's 1968 paper Functors of Artin rings identifies four conditions, traditionally numbered (H1)–(H4), characterising when $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ has a hull:

  • (H1) For surjections $A' \twoheadrightarrow A$ in $\mathrm{Art}_k$ and any $A'' \to A$, the natural map $\mathrm{Def}(A' \times_A A'') \to \mathrm{Def}(A') \times_{\mathrm{Def}(A)} \mathrm{Def}(A'')$ is surjective.
  • (H2) When $A = k$ and $A' \twoheadrightarrow k$ is a small extension, the map in (H1) is bijective.
  • (H3) $\dim_k \mathrm{Def}(D) < \infty$ — the tangent space of the functor is finite-dimensional.
  • (H4) The map in (H1) is bijective for arbitrary $A$ (this strengthens (H1)+(H2)). When (H4) holds along with (H1)–(H3), $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ is pro-representable by a complete local Noetherian $k$-algebra $R$.

A hull is a complete local $k$-algebra $R$ together with a smooth, surjective natural transformation $h_R \to \mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ inducing an isomorphism on tangent spaces. Schlessinger's theorem: $\mathrm{Def}_{X_0}$ has a hull iff (H1), (H2), and (H3) hold; it is pro-representable iff (H1)–(H4) all hold. For $X_0$ smooth projective, all four hold, and the hull lives at the heart of every deformation-space construction (Hilbert schemes, $\mathrm{Quot}$-schemes, moduli of stable bundles).

Worked example. The functor $h_R \colon \mathrm{Art}_k \to \mathrm{Set}$, $A \mapsto \Hom_k\text{-alg}(R, A)$, of any complete local $k$-algebra $R$ trivially satisfies (H1)–(H4); so a representable functor is its own hull. The interesting examples are deformation functors of singular schemes (a node, a cusp), where (H4) can genuinely fail and only a hull exists. The bridge to actual moduli stacks is then through Artin's algebraisation criteria.

5. The cotangent complex $L_{X/Y}$

The Čech-cohomology pictures of §1–3 see $T_{X_0} = \mathcal{H}om(\Omega^1_{X_0/k}, \mathcal{O}_{X_0})$ on the nose, but for $X_0$ singular the sheaf $\Omega^1_{X_0/k}$ doesn't carry enough information — squashing higher derived structure into a single sheaf loses obstruction data. Illusie (and, independently, Quillen) discovered the correct replacement: a complex $L_{X/Y}$ in the derived category $D^{\le 0}(\mathcal{O}_X)$ whose Ext groups recover the deformation invariants in a uniform way.

The construction starts from a general fact about commutative algebras. Given a morphism $f\colon X \to Y$, choose a "free resolution" of $\mathcal{O}_X$ as an $\mathcal{O}_Y$-algebra by polynomial algebras $P_\bullet$ (a simplicial $\mathcal{O}_Y$-algebra resolution). The cotangent complex

$L_{X/Y} \;:=\; \Omega^1_{P_\bullet/\mathcal{O}_Y} \otimes_{P_\bullet} \mathcal{O}_X$

is an object in $D^{\le 0}(\mathcal{O}_X)$, well-defined up to quasi-isomorphism. The construction is the algebraic analogue of "take a CW model and compute its tangent bundle" — the resolution $P_\bullet$ is a "smooth approximation" of $X \to Y$, even when $f$ itself is singular.

The structural facts that make this useful:

  • $f$ smooth $\Rightarrow$ $L_{X/Y}$ is concentrated in degree $0$ and equals $\Omega^1_{X/Y}[0]$. No new information beyond the relative differentials.
  • $f$ a regular embedding (l.c.i.) of codimension $r$ $\Rightarrow$ $L_{X/Y}$ has cohomology in degrees $0$ and $-1$: $\mathcal{H}^0 = \Omega^1_{X/Y}$, $\mathcal{H}^{-1} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{I}^2$ (the conormal sheaf).
  • Transitivity triangle. For $X \to Y \to Z$ there is a distinguished triangle $f^* L_{Y/Z} \to L_{X/Z} \to L_{X/Y} \to f^* L_{Y/Z}[1]$ — the derived analogue of the conormal exact sequence.
  • Deformation invariants. $\mathrm{Def}_{X/Y}(D) = \mathrm{Ext}^1_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L_{X/Y}, \mathcal{O}_X)$, the obstruction lives in $\mathrm{Ext}^2_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L_{X/Y}, \mathcal{O}_X)$. For $X/Y$ smooth this recovers $H^1(X, T_{X/Y})$ and $H^2(X, T_{X/Y})$ — sections 1–3 are the smooth case of the Ext interpretation.

The point is uniformity: even when $X_0$ is singular and $T_{X_0} = \mathcal{H}om(\Omega^1_{X_0}, \mathcal{O}_{X_0})$ is the "wrong" object, $\mathrm{Ext}^i(L_{X_0/k}, \mathcal{O}_{X_0})$ is always the right one. Read Illusie's two-volume Complexe cotangent et déformations (SLN 239 + 283) once and the obstruction-theoretic fog clears.

Worked example. For $X_0 = \Spec\,k[x]/(x^2)$ (a fat point), $L_{X_0/k}$ has $\mathcal{H}^0 = \Omega^1 = k \cdot dx /(2x\,dx) \cong k$ and $\mathcal{H}^{-1} = (x^2)/(x^4) \cong k \cdot \overline{x^2}$ — both 1-dimensional. The deformation problem has $\mathrm{Ext}^1(L, k) = k$ (one direction: $x^2 = t$, smoothing the fat point) and $\mathrm{Ext}^2 = 0$ (no obstruction), correctly capturing $\mathrm{Def}(\text{fat point}) \cong \Spec\,k[\![t]\!]$.

6. Worked example: deformations of a smooth curve

The cleanest concrete instance of the §1–5 machinery: a smooth projective curve $C$ over $k = \bar k$, of genus $g$. Two facts conspire to give a uniformly clean answer:

  • $\dim C = 1 \Rightarrow H^i(C, \mathcal{F}) = 0$ for any $i \ge 2$ and any coherent $\mathcal{F}$. In particular $H^2(C, T_C) = 0$, so curves are unobstructed — every first-order deformation extends to all orders.
  • Riemann–Roch + Serre duality compute $H^1(C, T_C)$ explicitly. $T_C$ is a line bundle of degree $2 - 2g$ (it is the dual of the canonical bundle $\Omega^1_C$, which has degree $2g - 2$). For $g \ge 2$, $\deg T_C = 2 - 2g < 0$, hence $H^0(C, T_C) = 0$ (no infinitesimal automorphisms). Riemann–Roch then gives

$\chi(T_C) = \deg T_C - g + 1 = (2 - 2g) - g + 1 = 3 - 3g$,

so $\dim H^1(C, T_C) = -\chi(T_C) = 3g - 3$. Equivalently, by Serre duality $H^1(C, T_C) \cong H^0(C, \Omega^1_C \otimes \Omega^1_C)^\vee = H^0(C, \Omega^{\otimes 2}_C)^\vee$ — the dual of the space of quadratic differentials, which has dimension $3g - 3$ by Riemann–Roch applied to $\Omega^{\otimes 2}_C$ (degree $4g - 4$).

So for $g \ge 2$ the moduli space $\mathcal{M}_g$ is smooth of dimension $3g - 3$ at every point. The boundary cases:

  • $g = 0$: $C = \mathbb{P}^1$, $T = \mathcal{O}(2)$, $H^1 = 0$. Rigid: $\mathcal{M}_0$ is a point.
  • $g = 1$: $C$ elliptic, $T_C = \mathcal{O}_C$, $H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \cong k$ has dimension $1$. $\mathcal{M}_{1,1}$ is one-dimensional (the $j$-line). Note $h^0(T_C) = 1$ as well — non-trivial automorphisms appear, foreshadowing why $\mathcal{M}_g$ for $g \le 1$ is best treated as a stack.
  • $g \ge 2$: $\dim \mathcal{M}_g = 3g - 3$, automorphism groups are finite, and $\mathcal{M}_g$ is a quasi-projective variety (Deligne–Mumford).

Worked example. For $g = 4$: $\dim \mathcal{M}_4 = 3(4) - 3 = 9$. A canonical curve of genus $4$ embeds via $\Omega^1$ into $\mathbb{P}^3$ as a degree-$6$ curve, the complete intersection of a quadric and a cubic. The "quadric or cubic" choice (whether the quadric is smooth or a cone) gives a stratification of $\mathcal{M}_4$ by trigonal versus general curves — the kind of geometric feature only made possible by knowing $\mathcal{M}_g$ is $9$-dimensional and smooth here.

params.bodyScript:
<script>
(function(){
  const svg = document.getElementById('svg-genus-tangent');
  const out = document.getElementById('dt-readout');
  const gEl = document.getElementById('dt-g');
  const gVal = document.getElementById('dt-g-val');
  if(!svg || !out || !gEl) return;

  function dimH1T(g){
    if(g === 0) return 0;        // P^1: rigid
    if(g === 1) return 1;        // elliptic: 1-dim
    return 3*g - 3;              // g >= 2: 3g - 3
  }
  function dimH0T(g){
    if(g === 0) return 3;        // sl_2 = aut(P^1)
    if(g === 1) return 1;        // translations
    return 0;                    // g >= 2: no infinitesimal autos
  }

  function draw(){
    svg.innerHTML = '';
    // re-create the markers definitions
    const defs = SVG('defs');
    const m = SVG('marker',{id:'dt-arr', viewBox:'0 0 10 10', refX:9, refY:5, markerWidth:6, markerHeight:6, orient:'auto'});
    m.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:'M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z', fill:'var(--ink)'}));
    defs.appendChild(m);
    svg.appendChild(defs);

    const g = +gEl.value;
    gVal.textContent = 'g = ' + g;

    // axes
    const x0 = 60, y0 = 180, xMax = 440, yMax = 30;
    const xPx = (gg) => x0 + (xMax - x0) * (gg / 10);
    const yPx = (dd) => y0 - (y0 - yMax) * (dd / 30);

    // axes lines
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:x0, y1:y0, x2:xMax+10, y2:y0, stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':1.2, 'marker-end':'url(#dt-arr)'}));
    svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:x0, y1:y0, x2:x0, y2:yMax-10, stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':1.2, 'marker-end':'url(#dt-arr)'}));
    // axis labels
    const xl = SVG('text',{x:xMax+12, y:y0+14, 'font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    xl.textContent = 'g'; svg.appendChild(xl);
    const yl = SVG('text',{x:x0-50, y:yMax-14, 'font-size':12, fill:'var(--mute)'});
    yl.textContent = 'dim'; svg.appendChild(yl);

    // tick labels for g
    for(let gg = 0; gg <= 10; gg += 2){
      const tx = xPx(gg);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:tx, y1:y0, x2:tx, y2:y0+4, stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':1}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:tx, y:y0+16, 'text-anchor':'middle', 'font-size':10, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = gg; svg.appendChild(t);
    }
    // tick labels for dim
    for(let dd = 0; dd <= 30; dd += 5){
      const ty = yPx(dd);
      svg.appendChild(SVG('line',{x1:x0-4, y1:ty, x2:x0, y2:ty, stroke:'var(--mute)','stroke-width':1}));
      const t = SVG('text',{x:x0-10, y:ty+4, 'text-anchor':'end', 'font-size':10, fill:'var(--mute)'});
      t.textContent = dd; svg.appendChild(t);
    }

    // plot the dim H^1(T_C) curve as data points
    for(let gg = 0; gg <= 10; gg += 1){
      const dd = dimH1T(gg);
      const cx = xPx(gg), cy = yPx(dd);
      const isHere = (gg === g);
      const c = SVG('circle',{cx, cy, r: isHere ? 7 : 4,
        fill: isHere ? 'var(--yellow)' : 'var(--blue)',
        stroke: isHere ? 'var(--ink)' : 'none',
        'stroke-width': 1.5});
      svg.appendChild(c);
    }
    // a 3g-3 reference line for g >= 2
    let pathStr = '';
    for(let gg = 2; gg <= 10; gg++){
      pathStr += (pathStr ? ' L ' : 'M ') + xPx(gg) + ' ' + yPx(3*gg - 3);
    }
    svg.appendChild(SVG('path',{d:pathStr, fill:'none', stroke:'var(--violet)','stroke-width':1.2, 'stroke-dasharray':'4 3'}));
    // label for the trend line
    const ll = SVG('text',{x:xPx(8)+8, y:yPx(3*8 - 3)-8, 'font-size':11, fill:'var(--violet)'});
    ll.textContent = 'y = 3g − 3 (g ≥ 2)';
    svg.appendChild(ll);

    // readout
    const dim1 = dimH1T(g), dim0 = dimH0T(g);
    let regime;
    if(g === 0) regime = 'rigid (P¹ has no moduli)';
    else if(g === 1) regime = 'elliptic curves: 1-parameter family (j-invariant)';
    else regime = `M_g smooth of dim 3g − 3 = ${3*g-3}`;

    out.innerHTML =
      `genus $g = ${g}$  |  ` +
      `$\\dim H^1(C, T_C) = ${dim1}$  |  ` +
      `$\\dim H^0(C, T_C) = ${dim0}$  |  ` +
      `$H^2 = 0$ — ${regime}`;
    if(window.renderMathInElement){
      renderMathInElement(out, {delimiters:[{left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},{left:'$',right:'$',display:false}], throwOnError:false});
    }
  }
  gEl.addEventListener('input', draw);
  draw();
})();
</script>

<script>
(function(){
  if(!window.MVInlineCodeCell) return;
  MVInlineCodeCell.init('#w-curve-sandbox', {
    title: "Curve-deformation sandbox: verify $h^1(C, T_C) = 3g - 3$",
    hint: "edit and Cmd/Ctrl+Enter to run · prelude: gcd, mod, factor, isPrime, primes, pow",
    initialCode: "// Verify the Riemann-Roch computation for h^1(C, T_C) on a smooth proj curve of genus g.
// T_C is the tangent line bundle, dual to the canonical Omega^1; deg T_C = 2 - 2g.
// Serre duality:  h^1(C, T_C) = h^0(C, Omega^(2)) = dim of quadratic differentials.
// Riemann-Roch on Omega^(2):  deg = 2(2g-2) = 4g-4, so for g >= 2 this is >= 2g-2 (the Serre bound)
// hence  h^0(Omega^(2)) = (4g-4) - g + 1 = 3g - 3.

function h1Tc(g){
  if (g === 0) return 0;        // P^1: rigid
  if (g === 1) return 1;        // elliptic: 1-dim
  return 3*g - 3;               // g >= 2: 3g - 3
}

// Compare to the chi(T_C) shortcut: chi = deg(T) - g + 1 = (2-2g) - g + 1 = 3 - 3g
// and h^0(T_C) = 0 for g >= 2, so h^1 = -chi = 3g - 3.
function h1TcViaChi(g){
  const degT = 2 - 2*g;
  const chi  = degT - g + 1;
  // for g >= 2:  h^0 = 0  =>  h^1 = -chi
  if (g >= 2) return -chi;
  if (g === 1) return 1;
  if (g === 0) return 0;
}

for (let g = 0; g <= 6; g++) {
  const a = h1Tc(g), b = h1TcViaChi(g);
  console.log('g = ' + g + ':  3g-3 formula = ' + a + ',  chi-route = ' + b + (a === b ? '  ok' : '  MISMATCH'));
}
",
    prelude: "math",
    rows: 14,
    runLabel: "Run",
  });
})();
</script>

blocks.html:
See also
  • Riemann–Roch on curves

7. Connections

Deformation theory studies what an object can become under infinitesimal perturbation. The places below are the standard receivers of its output.

Local algebra and Artin rings

First-order deformations of an object $X$ are $\Hom(\Omega^1, T_X)$ where $T_X$ is the tangent module; commutative algebra develops the local ring theory needed to interpret deformations over Artin local bases.

Schemes and obstruction theory

Smoothness of moduli at $[X]$ is the vanishing of $H^2$ of the tangent complex; schemes and sheaf cohomology supply the language. The pro-representable functor is exactly Schlessinger’s.

Moduli spaces

Moduli spaces and stacks are the global completions of formal deformation problems — sometimes representable as algebraic spaces, sometimes only as stacks — and a smooth moduli at $[X]$ is exactly when $H^2$ of the deformation complex vanishes.

Derived deformation theory

The right home for obstruction theory is the cotangent complex; derived categories and the dgla / $L_\infty$-algebra controlling a deformation problem are now the standard packaging.

Open frontiers: derived deformation theory (Lurie–Pridham: every formal moduli problem in characteristic 0 is the Maurer–Cartan locus of an $L_\infty$-algebra), shifted symplectic structures on derived moduli, and global deformation problems for Galois representations (Mazur’s deformation rings, $R = T$ theorems).
